channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - All Critical Infrastructure
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] CISA re-issues Iranian PLC-attack advisory as water, energy and government operators report OT disruption
item.description: CISA and partner agencies updated advisory AA26-097A on 22 July, broadening a campaign that Iran’s IRGC-linked CyberAv3ngers persona has run against internet-exposed programmable logic controllers across US critical infrastructure. The update adds detection guidance for malicious changes to reusable code modules inside Rockwell Automation PLC programs and expands confirmed targeting to Schneider Electric and Siemens controllers. Agencies say victims in the water and wastewater, energy and government sectors have already suffered operational disruption and financial loss after actors manipulated PLC project files and altered data shown on HMI and SCADA screens. For OT defenders the message is blunt: exposed PLCs remain the softest, highest-consequence target, and this is confirmed hands-on manipulation of control logic — not reconnaissance — spanning multiple sectors and multiple controller vendors. - CISA AA26-097A (updated 22 Jul 2026) / FBI / NSA / EPA / DOE — via CISA & Industrial Cyber - Updated 22 Jul 2026

item.title: [Critical] Ransomware halts all US production at Coca-Cola’s Fairlife dairy
item.description: Coca-Cola disclosed in a 16 July SEC 8-K that its Fairlife dairy subsidiary suffered a ransomware event involving unauthorized access to a portion of its systems, including production-related systems, and has temporarily suspended production at all of its US facilities. Fairlife — a roughly $4 billion-a-year brand — says Canadian operations and product safety are unaffected. For OT defenders this is the recurring pattern that matters most: even where attackers do not touch process controllers directly, ransomware in the plant IT and production-scheduling layer can force a full stop, and food & beverage lines with tight cold-chain and shelf-life constraints have little slack to absorb it. As of 17 July no group had claimed the attack and Coca-Cola had not confirmed data theft. - Coca-Cola SEC Form 8-K — via The Register & Cybernews - 16 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] NSA, CISA, FBI and 12 allied nations warn Russia’s FSB Center 16 is harvesting configs from critical-infrastructure routers
item.description: A joint advisory (AA26-194A, “Improve Router Hygiene”) attributes a long-running campaign to Russia’s FSB Center 16 — the actor also tracked as Berserk Bear / Dragonfly / Energetic Bear. The actors scan for internet-exposed networking devices running SNMP with default or weak community strings, then issue SNMP writes abusing the Cisco config-copy MIB to exfiltrate device configurations over TFTP; they also opportunistically exploit Cisco Smart Install and known device CVEs. Stolen configs expose network topology, credentials and VPN details — ideal reconnaissance for deeper access. Named sectors most at risk include Energy, Communications, Defense Industrial Base, Financial Services, Government (especially state/local) and Healthcare. For OT teams, the perimeter routers and firewalls fronting plant networks are exactly the under-audited devices in scope. - CISA AA26-194A / NSA / FBI — via Nextgov & Forbes - 13 Jul 2026

item.title: [Critical] SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days exploited in the wild — a favorite OT remote-access on-ramp under attack
item.description: SonicWall confirmed attackers are exploiting two zero-days in its SMA 1000 secure-access appliances: CVE-2026-15409, a critical (CVSS 10) server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface reachable by an unauthenticated remote attacker, and CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2), a post-authentication OS command-injection flaw in the management console. Rapid7’s MDR team observed the bugs used for stealthy initial access on internet-facing appliances — harvesting credentials, active session databases and TOTP MFA seeds — and Volexity has since tied the activity to a threat cluster it tracks as UTA0533. CISA added both to its KEV catalog on 14 July with a 17 July federal remediation deadline. SMA-class VPN/remote-access gateways are a common bridge into OT networks, and stolen sessions and MFA seeds are exactly what enables the credential-driven, living-off-the-land pivots defenders keep seeing. - CVE-2026-15409 · -15410 - 14 Jul 2026

item.title: [Critical] PTC Windchill RCE exploited in the wild — web shells landing on a manufacturing PLM backbone
item.description: CISA added CVE-2026-12569, a critical (CVSS 9.3) remote-code-execution flaw in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after the first confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. The bug stems from deserialization of untrusted data, and attackers are chaining it to deploy JSP web shells on exposed servers; PTC has warned of continued heightened threat activity even though patches shipped days earlier. Windchill is product-lifecycle-management software that sits deep in manufacturing engineering environments — holding CAD, BOM and process data and often bridging enterprise and plant networks — so a web shell here is a foothold with a clear line toward OT-adjacent assets. - CVE-2026-12569 - 26 Jun 2026

item.title: [Critical] Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited — the warning about OT edge gear plays out
item.description: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2025-67038, a vulnerability in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters that was disclosed in April as part of the BRIDGE:BREAK research project into serial device servers. These converters bolt legacy serial field equipment onto IP networks — a fixture in water, energy and manufacturing plants — and often sit at the OT edge with little monitoring. Exploitation followed earlier warnings that this class of device was an under-watched attack surface, and confirms the pattern: adversaries reach for the cheap, forgotten boxes that bridge serial and IP long before they touch a PLC. - CVE-2025-67038 - 25 Jun 2026

item.title: [High] Handala claims a Cal Water hack — Mandiant finds the OT untouched
item.description: Iran-linked group Handala claimed an attack on California Water Service (Cal Water) and published roughly 5 GB of data, including customer information and credentials for an RTKBase GNSS platform, framing it as retaliation for US military action. Cal Water brought in Mandiant, which concluded the activity was limited to unauthorized access to a small number of user accounts across two third-party service-provider platforms — with no evidence of any activity in the utility’s operational technology environment. It is a useful case in reading claims carefully: the group asserted it could disrupt water supply, but the confirmed impact was an IT/third-party data exposure, not OT control. - California Water Service / Mandiant — via SecurityWeek - 25 Jun 2026

item.title: [High] World Leaks dumps Reliance files tied to India’s largest nuclear plant
item.description: The extortion group World Leaks posted a large cache of files on its dark-web site that it attributed to India’s Reliance Group, including purported blueprints and supplier details connected to the Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant. Reliance — a contractor building infrastructure for Kudankulam Units 3 and 4 — confirmed a “partial breach” of data held on a server run by third-party data-center provider Yotta and said it had informed the government. The roughly 19,000 most sensitive files reportedly include ventilation and cooling-system drawings and a control-room floor layout, but not the Russian-supplied reactor core systems. This is a supply-chain data exposure rather than an OT compromise — but leaked engineering detail on a nuclear site shows how contractor and third-party data becomes an OT risk of its own. - Reliance Group / Reuters — via Business Standard & Al Jazeera - 15 Jul 2026

item.title: [Medium] Blackfield ransomware demands $2M from motor maker Nidec after breaching a Taiwanese subsidiary
item.description: The Blackfield ransomware crew has listed Nidec — the large Japanese electric-motor and industrial-component manufacturer — on its leak site, demanding $2 million and claiming it stole more than two terabytes of data from subsidiary Nidec Chaun-Choung Technology in Taiwan. The group says the archive spans employee, financial, procurement, manufacturing, legal and IT records, and is offering to sell or publish it if unpaid. Nidec states the subsidiary runs on an independent network that limited the intrusion’s reach and has not confirmed the volume or contents of any stolen files — the figures come from the attacker, which has every incentive to inflate them. No production impact has been reported, but it is another reminder that manufacturing remains the most-targeted sector for industrial ransomware. - Blackfield leak-site claim / Nidec — via BleepingComputer - 17 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Unit 42 chains three Siemens ROX II zero-days to root on industrial switches
item.description: Palo Alto Networks’ Unit 42 disclosed a three-vulnerability exploit chain in Siemens ROX II operating-system switches — ruggedized devices used to route traffic inside substation, rail and plant networks. Chained together (CVE-2025-40948, -40947 and -40949, the last rated CVSS 9.1), an attacker moves from arbitrary file disclosure through a feature-key privilege-escalation flaw to full, persistent root access on the device. Individually the bugs range from medium to critical; the value of the write-up is showing how ordinary configuration and logic weaknesses compose into device takeover, and how a compromised OT switch becomes a durable pivot for lateral movement across the very network it segments. Siemens has published advisories (SSA-973901, -078743, -081142) and fixes in ROX II firmware V2.17.1. - CVE-2025-40948 · -40947 · -40949 - 16 Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] Dragos unveils EmberAI — a defensive assistant built on its OT dataset
item.description: Dragos introduced EmberAI, an AI capability built on the large body of operational-technology telemetry, threat intelligence and incident-response data the company has accumulated. The pitch is to help analysts move faster through OT-specific investigation and triage — the domain where general-purpose security tooling tends to be weakest — rather than to automate response. It arrives directly against the backdrop of adversaries experimenting with commercial AI to pursue OT access, and reflects where the vendor conversation is heading: applying AI to the OT visibility and analyst-shortage problems, with humans kept firmly in the loop for any action that touches a physical process. - Dragos — via SecurityWeek - 23 Jun 2026

item.title: [Critical] July ICS Patch Tuesday: a CVSS 10 Siemens auth-bypass headlines a heavy month from the big three
item.description: Siemens, Rockwell Automation and Schneider Electric all shipped July advisories, and the volume matters as much as any single flaw. Siemens published nine advisories, six critical — including a CVSS 10 token-invalidation bug in Opencenter X that lets an attacker bypass authentication for full application access, plus critical fixes touching SIMATIC S7-1500, Mendix, Cadra and Desigo CC. Rockwell issued twelve advisories, two critical, one letting an unauthenticated attacker reach intrusive CLI commands on 1715 Redundant I/O. Schneider added an IGSS arbitrary-code-execution flaw and an EcoStruxure Cybersecurity Admin Expert authentication bypass. None are known to be exploited yet — but this gear sits at the core of energy, water and manufacturing plants, and the patch-to-exploit window keeps shrinking. - Siemens ProductCERT / Rockwell / Schneider PSIRT — via SecurityWeek - 14 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Siemens SICAM 8 grid-automation devices — DoS flaws and insecure OPC UA defaults
item.description: CISA advisory ICSA-26-197-05 covers multiple vulnerabilities across Siemens SICAM 8 products, including SICAM A8000 and EGS device firmware used in substation automation and RTU roles. An HTTP-accessible debugging interface can let an authenticated attacker crash the web process for a denial-of-service condition (CVE-2026-54798 through -54801), and the affected application ships with a default configuration that disables all OPC UA security mechanisms — leaving that channel open to unauthorized access and control unless hardened. The advisory flags impact to the Energy and Critical Manufacturing sectors; Siemens directs customers to firmware V26.20 / V26.20.0 or later. - CVE-2026-54798 · -54799 · -54800 · -54801 - 16 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] CISA flags Schneider Easergy protection relays and OpenPLC in early-July advisory batch
item.description: On 9 July CISA released three ICS advisories worth attention across energy and water. Schneider Electric’s Easergy MiCOM Px40 series protection relays — core to substation protection schemes — and Schneider’s PowerChute Serial Shutdown software were both flagged, alongside OpenPLC v3, the open-source PLC runtime used in water/wastewater and energy settings and increasingly common in labs and smaller utilities. Protection relays sit at the safety-critical layer of the grid, so relay firmware advisories deserve a fast read even when no exploitation is reported; OpenPLC deployments, often stood up outside formal asset management, are easy to overlook. - CISA ICS Advisories — 9 July 2026 batch - 09 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Hitachi Energy e-mesh EMS and PROMOD flagged in CISA’s 7 July advisories
item.description: CISA’s 7 July release of seven ICS advisories included two Hitachi Energy products — the e-mesh Energy Management System and PROMOD power-market simulation software — used by utilities for energy management and planning. The same batch covered Siemens Mendix Studio Pro and SINEC OS, Digi International PortServer TS and Digi One SP IA, a Hydro-Québec EV-charging backend, and Labcenter Proteus. Hitachi Energy products sit across generation, transmission and grid-edge operations, so its advisories tend to matter to power utilities even when severity varies — and they frequently reference third-party components that ripple into other vendors’ stacks. - CISA ICS Advisories — 7 July 2026 batch - 07 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Rockwell Logix controllers — CVSS 9.2 flaws force an unrecoverable fault, in CISA’s 16 July batch
item.description: CISA advisory ICSA-26-197-06 covers denial-of-service flaws in Rockwell Automation CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers, with CISA assigning a CVSS 9.2 base score to the most severe entries (CVE-2025-12012 and CVE-2025-11698): an unauthenticated, low-complexity network attacker can write invalid file data and drive a controller into a major non-recoverable fault (MNRF) that requires on-site personnel to physically recover the device. A companion advisory (ICSA-26-197-08) flags a DoS issue in the Flex 5000 adapter. Rockwell began pushing firmware on 14 July. These are workhorse controllers on discrete and process floors, so an availability hit that halts a line or batch is a real operational event even without code execution. - CISA ICSA-26-197-06 & -08 / Rockwell PSIRT - 16 Jul 2026

item.title: [Medium] CISA’s 21 July batch: a Tycon power monitor with a login-bypass and “physical safety risk,” plus Siemens CADRA and a cobot flaw
item.description: CISA’s newest set of ICS advisories (the ICSA-26-202 series, 21 July) is worth a scan even though nothing here is known to be exploited. The standout is Tycon Systems TPDIN-Monitor-WEB2 (ICSA-26-202-01), a networked power monitor whose web interface performs no server-side credential validation at login; CISA warns successful exploitation could expose credentials, disrupt connected infrastructure or manipulate physical equipment — an explicit physical-safety risk. The batch also covers Siemens CADRA and SIDIS Secured SmartPlug, while a separate advisory flags CVE-2026-8153, an OS-command-injection bug in Universal Robots’ PolyScope 5 controller software used on widely deployed collaborative robot arms. Together they span power, manufacturing and robotics — the kind of small, internet-adjacent OT devices that rarely make the asset inventory. - CVE-2026-8153 - 21 Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] NERC CIP-012-2 now in force — real-time data between control centers gets tighter protection
item.description: CIP-012-2 became effective 1 July 2026, strengthening requirements to protect real-time operational data exchanged between control centers against unauthorized disclosure, modification and loss of availability — closing gaps left by the earlier revision. It lands alongside CIP-003-9, whose enforcement began in April, which extends security-governance and vendor electronic-remote-access requirements into low-impact BES environments that historically saw minimal oversight. For registered entities the practical questions now are scope and evidence: which control-center links and low-impact assets are in play, and what documentation demonstrates compliance. Non-NERC operators can still borrow the intent — protect control-to-control communications and govern low-criticality remote access — as good practice. - NERC CIP-012-2 (effective 1 Jul 2026) / CIP-003-9 - Effective 01 Jul 2026
